subject: ap-psychology

core_concepts.title: Models of Memory
core_concepts.summary: The Atkinson-Shiffrin three-stage model proposes that information flows from sensory memory (fraction of a second) to short-term memory (STM, ~20 sec, 7±2 items) to long-term memory (LTM, potentially unlimited). Baddeley's working memory model expanded STM into a central executive, phonological loop, and visuospatial sketchpad. Long-term memory divides into explicit (declarative) memory — episodic (personal events) and semantic (facts/knowledge) — and implicit memory — procedural (skills), priming, and conditioning.

core_concepts.title: Encoding, Storage, and Retrieval
core_concepts.summary: Encoding transforms information into a memory trace through acoustic, visual, or semantic processing — semantic encoding produces the most durable memories (levels of processing, Craik and Lockhart). Storage maintains information over time. Retrieval accesses stored memories using cues; context-dependent memory and state-dependent memory show that encoding context improves retrieval. The testing effect (practice testing) and spacing effect (distributed practice) are powerful encoding strategies. Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve demonstrates exponential decay of memory over time without rehearsal.

core_concepts.title: Forgetting and Memory Errors
core_concepts.summary: Forgetting occurs through encoding failure (never stored), storage decay (trace fades), and retrieval failure (cue inadequacy). Retroactive interference occurs when new learning disrupts old memories; proactive interference occurs when old memories disrupt new learning. Elizabeth Loftus's research on the misinformation effect showed that post-event information can alter memories — eyewitness testimony is susceptible to suggestion. Source monitoring errors, the DRM paradigm (false memories for related words), and repressed memory controversies illustrate memory's reconstructive nature.

core_concepts.title: Thinking, Problem-Solving, and Decision-Making
core_concepts.summary: Algorithms guarantee correct solutions by testing all possibilities; heuristics are mental shortcuts that are efficient but error-prone. Availability heuristic judges likelihood by how easily examples come to mind; representativeness heuristic judges by how well something matches a prototype. Confirmation bias leads to seeking information that confirms existing beliefs. Mental set (Einstellung effect) involves applying an old strategy to a new problem — fixation. Functional fixedness prevents seeing objects as having new uses. Overconfidence is the tendency to overestimate accuracy of one's judgments.

core_concepts.title: Language and Thought
core_concepts.summary: Chomsky proposed that humans have an innate language acquisition device (LAD) and argued for universal grammar — all human languages share deep structural properties. Language development follows stages: cooing, babbling, one-word (holophrases), two-word telegraphic speech, and then complex grammar — with a critical period for language acquisition. The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis (linguistic relativity) proposes that language shapes thought — stronger forms claim language determines thought, weaker forms argue language influences it. Phonemes are the smallest sound units; morphemes are the smallest meaning units.

key_terms.definition: Brief, high-capacity storage of sensory information — iconic (visual, ~0.5s) and echoic (auditory, ~3-4s)
Temporary, limited-capacity store (~7±2 chunks, ~20 sec without rehearsal)
Relatively permanent, potentially unlimited memory store
Baddeley's active system for manipulating and working with information in the moment
Conscious, intentional memory for facts and events — includes episodic and semantic memory
Unconscious memory — includes procedural skills, priming, and conditioning
Personal autobiographical memories tied to specific times and places
General knowledge about the world not tied to personal experience
Implicit memory for skills and habits (riding a bike, playing piano)
Processing information into memory — acoustic, visual, or semantic
Encoding meaning — produces the deepest and most durable memories
Grouping items into larger meaningful units to expand working memory capacity
Repeating information to maintain it in STM or transfer to LTM
Distributed practice over time produces better long-term memory than massed practice
Retrieving information enhances long-term retention more than re-studying
Ebbinghaus's graph showing rapid initial memory loss that levels off over time
New learning disrupts recall of older information
Older learning disrupts recall of newer information
Post-event information alters memory of the original event (Loftus)
Mistaking the source of a memory — confusing imagination with real experience
Logical, step-by-step problem-solving procedure that guarantees a correct solution
Mental shortcut that enables quick, practical judgment but can produce errors
Estimating frequency/probability by how easily examples come to mind
Judging probability by how well something matches a prototype
Tendency to search for, interpret, and recall information confirming one's beliefs
Tendency to approach new problems with the same strategy that worked before
Inability to see an object being used for purposes other than its conventional function
Smallest distinct sound unit in a language
Smallest unit of language that carries meaning
Hypothesis that language influences or determines the way we think about the world

exam_tip: When comparing memory models on the AP exam, know Atkinson-Shiffrin vs. Baddeley's working memory. For questions about forgetting, distinguish between decay, interference (proactive vs. retroactive), and retrieval failure. For decision-making, know the specific examples that illustrate each heuristic — the availability heuristic (overestimating plane crash risk after news coverage) and representativeness heuristic (assuming a quiet bookish person is a librarian rather than a farmer) appear frequently. Loftus's misinformation effect is tested heavily in FRQ contexts about eyewitness testimony.
